metadata.title: Cyber Threat Taxonomy // 2026 Threat Landscape
metadata.author: jasonlayton.com
metadata.notes: Common attack stages are mapped to MITRE ATT&CK Enterprise tactics. Resource Development (TA0042) has been omitted as it does not involve active engagement with a victim network or system.

threat_categories.name: Phishing & Social Engineering
threat_categories.description: Deceptive communications (email, SMS, voice, social media) used to manipulate users into disclosing credentials, approving MFA prompts, transferring funds, or executing malware. Includes spear phishing, smishing, vishing, and AI-generated impersonation.

common-attack-stages.context: Harvesting target email addresses, organisational structure, and personal information to craft convincing lures.
Delivering phishing payloads or credential harvesting links via email, SMS, voice, or social media channels.
Victim executes malicious attachment, macro, or script triggered by the phishing lure.
Capturing credentials through fake login portals, MFA prompt bombing, or token theft.
Using legitimate cloud services for payload hosting, HTML smuggling, or obfuscated links to bypass email security controls.
Establishing C2 channels following successful payload execution to maintain remote access.

threat_categories.name: Business Email Compromise (BEC)
threat_categories.description: Targeted impersonation of executives, vendors, or partners to induce fraudulent payments or sensitive data disclosure. Often involves mailbox takeover and persistence within M365/Google Workspace.

common-attack-stages.context: Researching organisational hierarchy, vendor relationships, payment processes, and executive communication styles.
Gaining access to a legitimate mailbox via credential phishing, password spraying, or token theft.
Creating mailbox rules to redirect or hide emails, registering rogue OAuth applications, or adding MFA devices for sustained access.
Harvesting additional credentials or tokens from the compromised mailbox environment.
Monitoring email threads, collecting invoice templates, and gathering financial and contact information to support impersonation.
Using inbox rules to conceal attacker activity, spoofing display names, and leveraging legitimate mail infrastructure.
Initiating fraudulent wire transfers, redirecting payments to attacker-controlled accounts, or exfiltrating sensitive data.

threat_categories.name: Credential Theft & Identity Attacks
threat_categories.description: Theft, harvesting, cracking, or replay of authentication material (passwords, tokens, cookies, API keys). Includes MFA fatigue attacks, session hijacking, OAuth abuse, and pass-the-cookie/token replay.

common-attack-stages.context: Identifying authentication mechanisms, exposed login portals, and credential leak sources.
Using brute force, password spraying, or stolen credentials from dark web marketplaces to gain initial foothold.
Dumping credentials, extracting tokens and cookies, Kerberoasting, AS-REP roasting, or replaying captured session material.
Using valid credentials to blend with legitimate activity, bypassing MFA via token replay or fatigue attacks.
Leveraging stolen privileged credentials or tokens to escalate access within the environment.
Using harvested credentials and tokens to move across systems and services within the target environment.

threat_categories.name: Ransomware
threat_categories.description: Malware used to encrypt systems and/or exfiltrate data for extortion. Modern variants employ double or triple extortion (encryption + leak site + DDoS). Often delivered via initial access brokers (IABs).

common-attack-stages.context: Entry via phishing, exploitation of internet-facing services, or access purchased from initial access brokers.
Deploying ransomware payloads, loaders, and staging tools on compromised hosts.
Installing backdoors and establishing fallback access to maintain presence during the operation.
Escalating to domain admin or equivalent privileges to enable widespread deployment.
Disabling EDR/AV, deleting shadow copies, and using legitimate tools to avoid detection.
Dumping credentials to facilitate lateral movement and access to high-value targets.
Enumerating Active Directory, network shares, backup systems, and critical business assets.
Spreading across the environment to maximise the blast radius of encryption.
Staging sensitive data for exfiltration prior to encryption (double extortion).
Transferring stolen data to attacker infrastructure for use in extortion.
Maintaining C2 channels for operator-directed deployment and coordination.
Encrypting systems, destroying backups, and issuing ransom demands via leak sites.

threat_categories.name: Data Extortion (Unencrypted)
threat_categories.description: Theft of sensitive data without encryption, followed by extortion threats to publish or sell the data. Increasingly common due to faster execution and lower operational overhead than ransomware.

common-attack-stages.context: Gaining entry via credential theft, exploitation, or purchased access.
Identifying high-value data repositories, databases, and file shares containing sensitive information.
Aggregating and staging targeted data for extraction.
Transferring stolen data to attacker-controlled infrastructure, often using cloud storage services or encrypted channels.
Using legitimate tools and encrypted channels to avoid triggering DLP or network monitoring controls.
Threatening to publish or sell stolen data unless extortion demands are met.

threat_categories.name: Supply Chain Compromise
threat_categories.description: Compromise of software vendors, SaaS providers, MSPs, CI/CD pipelines, or open-source dependencies to gain indirect access to downstream victims. Includes malicious code injection and trusted update abuse.

common-attack-stages.context: Identifying target supply chain relationships, software dependencies, and build pipeline infrastructure.
Compromising a vendor, SaaS provider, or open-source package to inject malicious code into trusted software distribution channels.
Malicious code executes on downstream victim systems through trusted software updates or dependency imports.
Establishing persistent access within victim environments via backdoored software components.
Leveraging digitally signed, trusted software to bypass security controls and whitelisting policies.
Harvesting credentials, configuration data, and sensitive information from compromised downstream environments.
Using supply chain implants to establish covert C2 channels within victim networks.

threat_categories.name: Cloud Environment Intrusion
threat_categories.description: Exploitation of misconfigurations, exposed APIs, over-privileged IAM roles, or stolen cloud tokens to access IaaS/PaaS/SaaS environments. Often involves living-off-the-land abuse of native cloud services.

common-attack-stages.context: Scanning for exposed cloud storage, misconfigured APIs, and publicly accessible cloud management consoles.
Exploiting misconfigured IAM policies, exposed API keys, or stolen cloud tokens to gain access.
Exploiting over-permissive IAM roles, role chaining, or instance metadata services to escalate privileges.
Disabling cloud logging, using native cloud services to blend with normal operations, and modifying security group rules.
Extracting secrets from instance metadata, key vaults, or environment variables.
Enumerating cloud resources, subscriptions, storage accounts, and cross-account trust relationships.
Pivoting across cloud accounts, subscriptions, or tenants using compromised roles and trust relationships.
Exfiltrating data via cloud storage services, snapshots, or cross-account sharing.

threat_categories.name: API Abuse & Exploitation
threat_categories.description: Targeting insecure or poorly authenticated APIs to extract data, bypass controls, perform business logic abuse, or escalate privileges. Increasingly relevant in SaaS and fintech environments.

common-attack-stages.context: Discovering exposed API endpoints, reviewing API documentation, and identifying authentication weaknesses.
Exploiting broken authentication, insecure API keys, or exposed endpoints to gain unauthorised access.
Extracting API keys, tokens, or session identifiers through API enumeration or injection flaws.
Exploiting broken object-level or function-level authorisation to access privileged API functions.
Mass-harvesting data through insecure direct object references or excessive data exposure in API responses.
Extracting sensitive data at scale through repeated API calls or batch export functions.

threat_categories.name: Zero-Day Exploitation
threat_categories.description: Use of previously unknown vulnerabilities to achieve initial access or privilege escalation. Often weaponised rapidly against internet-facing appliances (VPNs, firewalls, hypervisors).

common-attack-stages.context: Identifying target technology stacks, firmware versions, and internet-facing appliances to select exploitable targets.
Exploiting the zero-day vulnerability to gain unauthorised access to the target system or network.
Executing arbitrary code on the compromised system via the exploited vulnerability.
Leveraging the exploit to gain elevated privileges, often to root or SYSTEM level on the target appliance.
Exploiting unknown vulnerabilities inherently evades signature-based detection; attackers may also tamper with device logs.
Deploying implants or web shells on compromised appliances to maintain access beyond initial exploitation.
Establishing covert communication channels from compromised edge devices back to attacker infrastructure.

threat_categories.name: N-Day Vulnerability Exploitation
threat_categories.description: Exploitation of known but unpatched vulnerabilities. Frequently automated at scale against exposed services.

common-attack-stages.context: Scanning the internet for services running vulnerable versions using mass scanning tools and Shodan/Censys.
Exploiting known vulnerabilities with publicly available exploit code against unpatched targets.
Executing payloads on compromised systems, often automated via exploitation frameworks.
Using known privilege escalation CVEs to elevate access on compromised hosts.
Deploying web shells, backdoors, or scheduled tasks to maintain access.
Modifying exploit techniques to bypass IDS/IPS signatures and patching detection gaps.

threat_categories.name: Initial Access Broker Activity
threat_categories.description: Specialised actors who gain footholds (via phishing, exploits, RDP brute force) and sell access to ransomware or espionage groups. Key enabler in modern criminal ecosystems.

common-attack-stages.context: Scanning for exposed RDP, VPN, and remote access services; identifying weak or reused credentials.
Gaining footholds via brute force, credential stuffing, phishing, or exploitation of internet-facing services.
Harvesting valid credentials and access tokens to package for resale.
Establishing durable access (backdoors, VPN accounts, web shells) that can be reliably handed off to buyers.
Performing basic enumeration to characterise the compromised environment and determine sale value.

threat_categories.name: Living-off-the-Land (LOTL) Techniques
threat_categories.description: Abuse of legitimate system tools (PowerShell, WMI, Azure CLI, AWS CLI, PsExec) to evade detection and blend with normal operations. Common in post-compromise phases.

common-attack-stages.context: Using native OS tools and scripting interpreters (PowerShell, WMI, Bash) for malicious execution.
Blending with legitimate administrative activity by using signed, trusted binaries and native cloud CLIs.
Abusing scheduled tasks, registry run keys, or cloud automation features for persistent access.
Leveraging built-in tools (net, nltest, dsquery, cloud CLI) to enumerate the environment without deploying custom tooling.
Using PsExec, WinRM, SSH, or cloud management APIs to move laterally using legitimate protocols.
Using native file management and compression tools to stage data for exfiltration.

threat_categories.name: Advanced Persistent Threat (APT) Operations
threat_categories.description: Long-term, covert campaigns typically linked to nation-states, focused on espionage, intellectual property theft, or strategic disruption. Characterised by stealth and persistence.

common-attack-stages.context: Extensive pre-compromise intelligence gathering on target organisations, personnel, and technology stacks.
Targeted spear phishing, zero-day exploitation, supply chain compromise, or watering hole attacks.
Deploying custom malware, implants, and modular frameworks tailored to the target environment.
Establishing multiple, redundant persistence mechanisms including firmware implants and covert scheduled tasks.
Exploiting local vulnerabilities or abusing trust relationships to achieve domain-wide or cloud-wide administrative access.
Employing custom tooling, code signing, LOTL techniques, and counter-forensics to maintain stealth over extended periods.
Targeting credential stores, Kerberos infrastructure, and cloud identity providers for long-term access.
Methodical and low-tempo enumeration of high-value assets, network topology, and data repositories.
Moving through the environment using compromised credentials, RDP, SMB, and cloud trust relationships.
Systematically identifying and staging targeted intelligence, IP, and strategic data.
Exfiltrating data through covert channels, encrypted tunnels, or cloud storage services over extended timeframes.
Maintaining resilient, multi-layered C2 infrastructure with domain fronting, encrypted protocols, and failover mechanisms.

threat_categories.name: Cyber Espionage
threat_categories.description: Targeted theft of sensitive government, defence, commercial, or research data for strategic advantage. May leverage zero-days and supply chain compromise.

common-attack-stages.context: Identifying high-value intelligence targets, personnel with access to classified or sensitive data, and technical infrastructure.
Gaining entry through targeted phishing, zero-day exploitation, or compromised trusted relationships.
Establishing long-term covert access through implants, backdoors, and compromised identity infrastructure.
Using advanced anti-forensics, encrypted communications, and LOTL techniques to avoid detection.
Carefully mapping network resources, file shares, and databases containing targeted intelligence.
Systematically harvesting documents, communications, research data, and strategic information.
Exfiltrating stolen intelligence through covert channels, often over extended periods to avoid detection.
Operating resilient C2 infrastructure designed for stealth and long-term operational security.

threat_categories.name: Distributed Denial of Service (DDoS)
threat_categories.description: Flooding services with traffic to degrade or disrupt availability. Includes volumetric, application-layer, and extortion-based DDoS. Often combined with other attacks.

common-attack-stages.context: Identifying target infrastructure, IP ranges, and services to determine the most effective attack vectors.
Compromising IoT devices, servers, and cloud instances to build or rent botnet infrastructure for the attack.
Coordinating botnet nodes to synchronise attack traffic against target infrastructure.
Overwhelming target services with volumetric or application-layer traffic to cause service degradation or outage.

threat_categories.name: Insider Threat
threat_categories.description: Malicious or negligent actions by employees, contractors, or partners that result in data theft, sabotage, or exposure. Includes privilege abuse and data exfiltration.

common-attack-stages.context: Leveraging legitimate access to identify sensitive data repositories, systems, and processes.
Accumulating sensitive data using authorised access, often over an extended period.
Abusing existing access or exploiting trust to gain access to systems or data beyond authorised scope.
Using legitimate credentials and normal work patterns to avoid triggering security alerts or DLP controls.
Transferring data via personal email, removable media, cloud storage, or other channels outside corporate monitoring.
Sabotaging systems, deleting data, or exposing sensitive information, whether through malice or negligence.

threat_categories.name: Malware (Generalised)
threat_categories.description: Software designed to disrupt, damage, or gain unauthorised access. Includes trojans, loaders, RATs, spyware, info-stealers, and botnet agents.

common-attack-stages.context: Delivering malware via phishing, drive-by downloads, malvertising, or exploitation of vulnerable services.
Executing malicious payloads on victim systems through user interaction or automated exploitation.
Installing mechanisms to survive reboots and maintain presence, such as registry modifications, services, or boot kits.
Employing packing, obfuscation, polymorphism, and process injection to evade antivirus and EDR detection.
Communicating with attacker infrastructure for command execution, updates, and data transfer.
Disrupting system operations, corrupting data, or enabling further attack objectives.

threat_categories.name: Info-Stealer Campaigns
threat_categories.description: Commodity malware designed specifically to harvest credentials, browser cookies, crypto wallets, and session tokens for resale on dark markets. Major enabler of downstream compromise.

common-attack-stages.context: Distributing info-stealers via malvertising, SEO poisoning, cracked software, phishing emails, or social media lures.
Executing the stealer payload, often via deceptive installers or script-based loaders.
Harvesting stored passwords, browser cookies, session tokens, crypto wallet data, and autofill information.
Aggregating stolen credential logs and session data for packaging.
Transmitting harvested data to attacker infrastructure, often via Telegram bots or dedicated C2 panels.
Using short-lived, polymorphic payloads and legitimate hosting to evade detection and takedown.

threat_categories.name: Cryptojacking
threat_categories.description: Unauthorised use of computing resources (often cloud workloads or containers) to mine cryptocurrency.

common-attack-stages.context: Exploiting misconfigured containers, exposed APIs, or compromised credentials to gain access to compute resources.
Deploying cryptocurrency mining software on compromised hosts or cloud instances.
Embedding miners in container images, cron jobs, or auto-scaling configurations to maintain mining operations.
Throttling CPU usage, using process name masquerading, and killing competing miners to avoid detection.
Consuming computational resources, increasing cloud costs, and degrading performance of legitimate workloads.

threat_categories.name: Operational Technology (OT) / ICS Attacks
threat_categories.description: Targeting industrial control systems, critical infrastructure, or manufacturing environments. May involve IT-to-OT pivoting.

common-attack-stages.context: Mapping IT/OT network boundaries, identifying SCADA/ICS assets, and researching industrial protocols and vendor systems.
Compromising IT network assets as a stepping stone, or exploiting internet-exposed OT devices and remote access solutions.
Pivoting from the IT network into OT/ICS segments through poorly segmented network boundaries.
Enumerating PLCs, RTUs, HMIs, and engineering workstations to understand the control environment.
Gathering process data, engineering schematics, and configuration files from OT environments.
Deploying ICS-specific malware or issuing rogue commands to programmable controllers.
Masquerading as legitimate engineering traffic and manipulating historian data to conceal attack activity.
Disrupting industrial processes, causing safety incidents, or damaging physical equipment through manipulated control logic.

threat_categories.name: IoT Exploitation
threat_categories.description: Compromise of insecure Internet of Things devices for botnets, surveillance, or lateral movement.

common-attack-stages.context: Scanning for exposed IoT devices using default credentials, known firmware vulnerabilities, and weak protocols.
Exploiting default credentials, unpatched firmware, or insecure management interfaces to compromise IoT devices.
Deploying botnet agents, surveillance implants, or proxy software on compromised devices.
Flashing modified firmware or embedding persistent malware that survives device reboots.
Using compromised IoT devices as pivot points to access internal network segments.
Enrolling compromised devices into botnet infrastructure for coordinated attacks or proxy services.
Using compromised device fleets for DDoS attacks, credential stuffing, or surveillance operations.

threat_categories.name: Deepfake & AI-Enabled Fraud
threat_categories.description: Use of AI-generated voice/video impersonation for financial fraud, executive impersonation, or social engineering escalation.

common-attack-stages.context: Collecting voice samples, video footage, and behavioural patterns of impersonation targets from public sources.
Using AI-generated voice or video to impersonate trusted individuals in real-time calls or pre-recorded messages.
Generating highly convincing synthetic media that bypasses human verification and voice-based authentication systems.
Inducing fraudulent financial transactions, authorising illegitimate actions, or manipulating decision-makers.

threat_categories.name: Financial System Manipulation
threat_categories.description: Attacks targeting payment platforms, trading systems, SWIFT infrastructure, or digital assets. Includes crypto exchange compromise.

common-attack-stages.context: Researching target financial systems, payment workflows, trading platforms, and blockchain infrastructure.
Compromising financial platform credentials, exploiting application vulnerabilities, or social engineering operations staff.
Escalating access to transaction approval systems, admin consoles, or smart contract management interfaces.
Manipulating transaction logs, modifying audit trails, and timing attacks to coincide with high-volume periods.
Gathering financial data, private keys, transaction patterns, and account information.
Executing fraudulent transactions, draining wallets, manipulating trades, or disrupting payment processing.

threat_categories.name: Web Application Attacks
threat_categories.description: Exploitation of application vulnerabilities such as SQL injection, XSS, SSRF, RCE, and authentication bypass.

common-attack-stages.context: Scanning web applications for input validation flaws, exposed admin panels, and technology stack fingerprinting.
Exploiting web application vulnerabilities (SQLi, RCE, authentication bypass) to gain unauthorised access.
Achieving remote code execution through injection flaws, deserialisation vulnerabilities, or uploaded web shells.
Extracting credentials from databases via SQL injection or accessing stored secrets through SSRF.
Exploiting authentication bypass or insecure direct object references to access administrative functions.
Obfuscating payloads to bypass WAF rules, using encoded injection techniques, and operating through legitimate HTTP traffic.
Extracting sensitive data from application databases, file systems, and connected backend services.

threat_categories.name: Business Logic Abuse
threat_categories.description: Manipulation of legitimate workflows (refund abuse, API chaining, privilege escalation via logic flaws) without necessarily exploiting technical vulnerabilities.

common-attack-stages.context: Analysing application workflows, business processes, and API sequences to identify exploitable logic assumptions.
Using legitimate user accounts or registering new accounts to interact with target applications.
Exploiting logic flaws to access functions, data, or privileges beyond the intended user scope.
Operating entirely within legitimate application parameters, making detection through traditional security controls extremely difficult.
Causing financial losses through refund abuse, inventory manipulation, or unauthorised transactions via logic exploitation.

threat_categories.name: Destructive Attacks (Wipers)
threat_categories.description: Malware designed to permanently destroy data or render systems inoperable, often geopolitically motivated.

common-attack-stages.context: Gaining entry through supply chain compromise, credential theft, or exploitation of internet-facing services.
Deploying wiper malware designed to overwrite or corrupt critical data and system components.
Escalating to administrative privileges required to access and destroy critical systems and data at scale.
Propagating through the network to maximise the destructive reach before triggering the wiper payload.
Disguising wiper malware as ransomware or legitimate tools to delay response until maximum damage is achieved.
Permanently destroying data, corrupting boot records, and rendering systems inoperable.

threat_categories.name: Hybrid Influence Operations
threat_categories.description: Combined cyber intrusion and information warfare campaigns designed to influence public opinion, elections, or market behaviour.

common-attack-stages.context: Identifying target audiences, media ecosystems, political fault lines, and information channels susceptible to manipulation.
Compromising media outlets, social media accounts, or government systems to obtain material for disinformation campaigns.
Stealing communications, documents, and data that can be weaponised or selectively released for maximum impact.
Using fake personas, bot networks, and laundered narratives to obscure attribution and amplify disinformation.
Leaking stolen data through intermediary platforms or media channels to control the narrative.
Undermining public trust, manipulating elections, influencing policy decisions, or destabilising markets through coordinated information operations.
